coredata.dc:title: Effective and selective removal of heavy metals from industrial effluents using sustainable Si–CD conjugate based column chromatography
coredata.dc:creator: Ajith, M.P.
Priyadarshini, Eepsita
Rajamani, Paulraj
coredata.dc:description: The present investigation deals with the green synthesis of aqueous-stable and highly fluorescent carbon dots (CD) by hydrothermal treatment of tender leaf extract of Ficus benghalensis. The synthesized CD was characterized. Carbon dots were of an average size of 2.28 nm with a blue-green fluorescence emission at 317 nm and showed high selectivity and specificity for iron and nickel amongst the different tested heavy metals with a LOD of 0.0015 μmol/mL and 0.000014 μmol/mL respectively. Further, we functionalized silica with the prepared carbon dot to generate an adsorbent for purification of contaminated water. A short bed adsorbed column system was designed for determining the efficiency of adsorption. As envisioned approximately, 77% and 74% removal of Fe and Ni was observed when the metal salts were eluted individually. Purification efficacy was analysed using an industrial electroplating effluent, which showed adsorption of 74% and 79% for Fe and Ni respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Heavy metal adsorption
Silica-carbon dot
Short bed adsorbed columns
Electroplating effluent
Bio adsorption
Column purification

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of heavy metals using synthesised CD
The selectivity of CD towards heavy metals

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The selectivity of CD with heavy metal. (a) Colour change after treatment (b) Fluorescence quenching by heavy metal. (c) Change in hydrodynamic diameter with heavy metal Treatment.
SBA column packed with Si-CD conjugates showing percentage removal of Ni and Fe in control set, individual metal treated, the mixture of Fe and Ni and industrial effluent.
Summary of hydrothermal synthesis of carbon dot using different sources.

head.ce:title: Effective and selective removal of heavy metals from industrial effluents using sustainable Si–CD conjugate based column chromatography

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental Sciences, Jawaharlal Nehru University, New Delhi 110067, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environmental Science, Jawaharlal Nehru University, New Delhi 110067, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The present investigation deals with the green synthesis of aqueous-stable and highly fluorescent carbon dots (CD) by hydrothermal treatment of tender leaf extract of Ficus benghalensis. The synthesized CD was characterized. Carbon dots were of an average size of 2.28 nm with a blue-green fluorescence emission at 317 nm and showed high selectivity and specificity for iron and nickel amongst the different tested heavy metals with a LOD of 0.0015 μmol/mL and 0.000014 μmol/mL respectively. Further, we functionalized silica with the prepared carbon dot to generate an adsorbent for purification of contaminated water. A short bed adsorbed column system was designed for determining the efficiency of adsorption. As envisioned approximately, 77% and 74% removal of Fe and Ni was observed when the metal salts were eluted individually. Purification efficacy was analysed using an industrial electroplating effluent, which showed adsorption of 74% and 79% for Fe and Ni respectively.

ce:section.ce:para: Industrialization and other anthropogenic activities result in the discharge of waste and wastewater containing heavy metals to the environment. The released heavy metals pose a significant risk to the ecosystem and public health because of their persistence and toxicity (Seiler et al., 1988). Metals such as Co, Cr, Mn, Fe, Zn, Cu, and Ni, are required for biological metabolism in trace amounts however, their higher dose may cause toxic effects. While, heavy metals as Cd, Pb, Hg, and As are not suitable for biological functions and are positively toxic. Cr (VI), Cu, Ni, and Zn are the heavy metals commonly found in wastewater discharges. The improper disposals of effluents result in the accumulation of such heavy metals in soil and water resulting in biomagnification and enter the food chain (Priyadarshini et al., 2019). The toxicological effects of these elements are of considerable concern because of their environmental burden and are an important problem in both developing and developed countries all over the world (Danazumi and Bichi, 2010; Inglezakis et al., 2007; Momodu and Anyakora, 2010). As many of the elements released in industrial effluent are stable, they are bio-accumulative, and assessment of their safe limits is a difficult task in the ecosystem. Further, the discard of the industrial effluents in water bodies results in contamination of water resources raising concerns on the supply of clean drinking water.

Different methods have been developed for the removal of heavy metals from wastewater which includes adsorption, chemical precipitation or coagulation, electrolytic reduction, ion exchange, and membrane technology. Low-cost and eco-friendly technologies including biosorbents, bioelectrochemical systems, bioleaching, and biomineralization, have become substitutes in the retrieval of critical metals (Yu et al., 2020). Adsorption is one of the most commonly used methods for the removal of heavy-metal ions because of its convenience, simplicity, and high efficiency (Stafiej and Pyrzynska, 2007; Santos Yabe and de Oliveira, 2003). Adsorption using activated carbon is attractive due to its effectiveness of absorbing heavy-metal ions even in trace quantities (Rao et al., 2009). However, the process has not been used extensively because of its high cost (Rao et al., 2009; Dias et al., 2007). Therefore, the use of alternative low-cost materials as potential adsorbents for the removal of heavy metal ions has been emphasized recently (Gupta et al., 2009).

Nanoparticles and nano-based formulations are recently being investigated for the removal of chemical pollutants, toxic dyes, heavy metal ions and have thus emerged as an interesting area of research. Compared to conventional adsorbents with micrometre size, nanomaterials offer a significantly higher surface-area-to-volume ratio and short diffusion route, resulting in high adsorbent capacity, superior removal efficiencies, and rapid removal dynamics (Klabunde et al., 2006). Carbon dots (CD) recently emerge as superior fluorophores due to its distinctive combination of numerous key virtues, including excellent photostability, minuscule size, biocompatibility, highly tunable photoluminescence (PL) property, exceptional multi‐photon excitation (up‐conversion) property, electrochemiluminescence, functionalized ease, and chemical inertness. Researches have investigated the use of several sources as fruits, plants, vegetables, coffee, milk etc for hydrothermal synthesis of CD. However, the high aqueous soluble nature of CD makes it difficult when used in water purification. Therefore, it is essential to conjugate an efficient adsorbent with CD for application in the purification process. Functionalized adsorbent as nanoconjugates can be used in aqueous as well as solid-phase purification. Solid absorbents as silica, activated carbon and graphene are widely investigated. Silica is extensively used as an adsorbent due to its advantages of chemical stability, granularity and porosity. It has been used as nanoconjugates with glycine and reduced glutathione (Mahmoun et al., 2018) for adsorption studies. Silica is also used in an embedded form with guar gum for adsorptive removal of metal ions and cationic dyes (Patra et al., 2017).

In the present study, the efficiency of heavy metal removal from industrial effluents by using the Silica-CD conjugates system as adsorbent via Small Bed Adsorber (SBA) column method was investigated. Biogenic CD was synthesized by microwave-assisted hydrothermal method from the leaf extract of Ficus benghalensis. Heavy metal-binding efficiency and adsorption affinity of the CD was evaluated by spectroscopy methods (UV–Visible spectrophotometer, fluorescence spectrophotometer and photon correlation spectrophotometer). The CD showed selective affinity towards Iron (Fe) and Nickel (Ni) and therefore, their adsorption kinetics was analysed. Moreover, a column-based purification strategy was developed by conjugation of CD with silica particles. The Silica-CD conjugates formed was packed in glass columns and analysed for removal of heavy metals from industrial effluents. The adsorption capacity and removal efficiency were also evaluated.

Tender leaves of Ficus benghalensis were collected from Jawaharlal Nehru University campus, New Delhi, India. Silica powder and metal salts (ZnCl2, CdCl2, NiCl2, FeCl3, MnCl2, CrCl2, PbCl2 and SbCl3) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Pvt. Ltd. Standard solution of Fe and Ni were procured from Agilent Technologies India Pvt. Ltd. New Delhi. Milli Q water was used in all experiment performance in this study.

CD was synthesised via microwave-assisted method involving hydrothermal carbonization of aqueous leaf extract of Ficus benghalensis. Briefly, 5 g of fresh leaves was homogenised using a motor and pestle by addition of 25 ml of Milli Q water. Homogenate was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. Pellet was discarded and the supernatant was used for CD synthesis. 5 ml of supernatant was taken in a beaker and transferred to a microwave reaction system operating at 350 °C for 2 min. After the reaction, the obtained CD was cooled to room temperature (RT) and 5 ml of Milli Q water was added. The synthesised CD was filtered using a 0.2 μm syringe filter.

The purified CD was subjected to several analytical characterizations. UV– Vis absorption spectrum was recorded in a wavelength range of 250 to 600 nm using Cary 60 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Agilent, USA). Photoluminescence was recorded on a single beam Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (Agilent, USA) at an excitation wavelength of 317 nm. The hydrodynamic radius (Rh) was determined using Photochor dynamic light scattering (DLS) instrument at θ = 90° and wavelength 647 nm. The Rh was calculated using the Stokes-Einstein equation (Priyadarshini and Rawat, 2017). Zeta potential measurement was performed on a ZEECOM instrument (Microtech, Japan), operating in the range of −200 to 200 mV. The colloidal CD solution was appropriately diluted in sterile distilled water and sonicated before analysis. The pH of the solution was maintained at 6.7 and 100 particles were collected in one phase. Histogram of zeta potential versus particle intensity was plotted. The functional groups embedded on CDs surface were determined by FTIR analysis using Agilent Cary 630 FTIR in the range of 400–4000 cm−1. For TEM study, the CD was drop coated onto a carbon-coated copper disc and after drying, images were taken on JEOL 2100F TEM instrument operating at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of heavy metals using synthesised CD
ce:section.ce:para: Stock solutions of respective metal salts (ZnCl2, CdCl2, NiCl2, FeCl3, MnCl2, CrCl2, PbCl2 and SbCl3) were prepared. The CD was treated with a respective metal solution in the ratio of 1:2 such that the final concentration of metal in the reaction system was 100 ppm. UV– visible absorption, fluorescence analysis and hydrodynamic diameter of the reaction mixture were measured at frequent intervals. Synthesized CD showed a high affinity for iron (Fe) and nickel (Ni). Further, sensitivity study in a concentration dependant manner was performed by treating CD with gradually increasing concentration of Fe and Ni salts respectively. The binding efficiency and selectivity of interaction were analysed using UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra.

For water purification, a mesoporous bed material was synthesized for which silica was used as the solid substrate and treated with CD to form silica-CD conjugate material. Briefly, 5 ml of CD (20 mg w/v) was treated with 2 g (w/w) of silica and allowed to interact for an hour under the constant stirring condition at 30 °C. The solution was then centrifuged. The desired silica-CD conjugate obtained in the pellet was characterized and used for purification studies. FTIR study was performed to elucidate the mechanism behind conjugation. Absorption spectra of CD before treatment and that of the supernatant obtained after centrifugation was recorded to determine the efficiency of silica binding to the CD.

To predict the efficacy of Silica-CD conjugate for industrial effluent purification a fixed-bed column was prepared, after a series of optimization steps. For the experimental investigation, Small Bed Adsorber (SBA) column was prepared using a glass column of length 14 cm and inner diameter 1.3 cm. The prepared Silica-CD conjugate was packed in the glass column supported by glass wool. Four similar SBA columns were prepared for individual parameters to be studied and control with silica only as the packing material was taken. The detail of the column operating conditions has been summarized in Table 1 .

To check the potential of silica-CD SBA, electroplating industrial effluent is taken as a real sample analysis. The effluent was collected from a local industry at Ghaziabad, Delhi NCR. The metal salts (FeCl3 and NiCl2) were used at 500 ppm (8.95 μmol/mL and 8.52 μmol/mL respectively) concentration. The solutions were passed through the respective column under controlled flow rate and fractions collected for a defined period. For control set, only silica was taken as adsorbent and mixture of heavy metal (NiCl2 + FeCl3) was allowed to flow through. The concentration of respective metal salts (Ni and Fe) in the collected fractions was analysed by Atomic Absorption Spectrophotometer (AAS) (Thermo Electron Corporation, UK) for all samples. All the experiments were performed at an actual pH.

The efficiency of treatment (Ni and Fe removal efficiency) was determined by using equation (1)
(1)
Removal
E f f i c i e n c y =
C 1 - C 2
C 1
× 100
where C1 = Initial Concentration; C2 = Concentration after Treatment.

CD in the present study was generated by green synthesis protocol using aqueous leaf extract of Ficus benghalensis via the microwave-assisted method. The green colour leaf extract changed to yellowish on microwave treatment, indicating generation of CD. Irradiation with UV-light showed high luminescence property of formed CD evident by blue-green colour. Microwave irradiation results in carbonization of protein and carbohydrates present in leaf extract of Ficus benghalensis which is in accordance to the literature suggesting that C and O groups of carbohydrates exist in a form that favours easy dehydration under hydrothermal conditions (Siddique et al., 2018). The UV visible spectra show an absorbance peak at 317 nm corresponding to CD, while the peak at 280 nm arises is due to the π – π* transition of aromatic amino acids of the protein present in leaf extract (Tripathi et al., 2017). A Photoluminescence spectrum of CDs obtained by exciting the CDs at 317 nm. Emission peak is displayed at 455 nm in the green region of absorption spectrum which is following blue-green luminescence of CD. Table 2 shows different sources used in the hydrothermal synthesis of CD.

Minute black dots observed in the TEM image indicates the CD formed from leaf extract. CD show narrow size distribution of 2–3 nm as determined from particle size distribution analysis. HRTEM images did not show any distinct lattice structures of CD signifying that the resultant CD is amorphous. An earlier study suggests that most of the synthesized CD posse’s weak crystallinity (Hassan et al., 2018). Zeta potential value was analysed to be −25 mV, which suggest very high stability of formed CD. Further, the mean hydrodynamic radius (Rh) calculated was 2.2 nm, which is in agreement with the TEM analysis.

FTIR spectra of CDs shows a strong broad peak at 3360 cm−1 which correspond to the O–H stretch of alcohol. A minor peak at 2835 cm−1 indicates the C–H stretch of aldehyde. A minor peak at 1876 cm−1 indicates C–H bending of aromatic compounds. The medium-sized three strong peaks at 1627 cm−1, 1422 cm−1 and 1093 cm−1 indicate the CC stretch of alkenes, O–H bending of alcohol and C-O stretch of alcohol respectively. A minor peak at 824 cm−1 indicates CC bending of the alkene.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The selectivity of CD towards heavy metals
ce:section.ce:para: Absorption and fluorescence spectra of the reaction mixture (i.e. CD treated with respective metal ions) were used as the basis to determine the efficacy of CD to bind heavy metal cations. Synthesized CD shows a high affinity towards Ni and Fe amongst the treated metal salts. After Fe treatment, the colour of the solution has changed into pale brown and with Ni treatment, the solution changed into dark brown aggregated clumps whereas the rest of the metal treatment did not show any noticeable colour change (Fig. 1 a). A distinct variation in the mode of interaction of CD was observed, while the reaction mixture containing Ni shows a blue shift of absorption peak, that of Fe showed a redshift. For all other studied metal ions, the CD retained the absorption peak at the original position (317 nm). Similarly, fluorescence quenching was comparatively higher for Ni and Fe, compared to other metal ions (Fig. 1b). Further, the optical detection analysis was following the DLS analysis which showed an increase in Rh value for Ni and Fe. The Rh was calculated to be 12.84 nm and 11.54 nm after treatment with Ni and Fe respectively (Fig. 1c). The shifting in absorption spectra accompanied by fluorescence quenching signify the superior affinity of CD for Ni and Fe which was also evident from the fluorescence correlation plot graph. It is thus suggestive of the fact that negatively charged CD crosslink with Ni2+ and Fe3+ ions resulting in aggregation and corresponding destabilization which leads to quenching (Priyadarshini and Rawat, 2017).

Further, sensitivity studies were performed with gradually increasing concentration of Ni and Fe to analyse the Limit of Detection (LOD) of the synthesized CD. A bathochromic shift seen in CD absorption peak along with gradual broadening was observed with increasing Fe. Calibration curve showed a linear relationship between aggregation ratio (λshifted / λCD) and that of Fe in the range of 0.14 μmol/mL to 5 μmol/mL with R2 value 0.99. Aggregation ratio represents the quantitative degree of agglomeration of metal ions after treatment with CD (Priyadarshini and Pradhan, 2017). Treatment of CD with progressively increasing concentration of Fe showed quenching in fluorescence. UV–Visible absorption spectra of the CD on Ni treatment showed a gradual blue shift along with decreased OD signifying their interaction. The calibration curve showed a linear relationship between aggregation ratio (λshifted / λCD) and that of Ni in the range of 0.14 μmol/mL to 4 μmol/mL with R2 value 0.96. Fluorescence quenching was also observed; however, the degree of quenching was quite less compared to that observed with Fe. The LOD was observed to be 0.0015 μmol/mL (0.0021 µl) and 0.000014 μmol/mL (0.0002 µl) for Fe and Ni respectively. The R2 value obtained from the correlation curve (Ni and Fe) indicated negative and positive correlation signifying a contrasting mode of interaction for the two metals. The fluorescence quenching graph of the CD on treatment with Ni shows that the quenching efficiency was less compared to that with Fe suggesting higher affinity of CD to bind to Fe3+ compared to Ni2+.

Silica modified CD was prepared based on the chemical crosslinking phenomenon by allowing them to interact. The suspension was centrifuged to remove any unconjugated CD particles. The obtained supernatant was light yellowish due to the adsorption of CD with silica particles. UV–Visible absorption spectra showed a shift in the CD absorption peak, signifying interaction. It was observed that after the interaction, the colour of silica changed from white to yellow. To confirm this, FTIR analysis of the conjugates was performed. FTIR spectral data signified a stable interaction between the silica and CD counterpart. The sharp peak at 1099 cm−1 in the FTIR spectrum of silica arises from the Si – O – Si asymmetrical stretching (Zemnukhova et al., 2015) which completely disappears after treatment with CD. CD shows a peak at 1422 cm−1 from the –OH bending (Coates, 2000) which facilitates conjugation with silica. The chemistry of interaction between silica and CD has been represented below.

The efficiency of short bed adsorbed columns (SBA), which represents a fixed-bed column of smaller length have been reported earlier (Smith and Weber, 1988). SBA function by stimulating adsorption in the upper region of column bed thereby assisting in the analysis of limiting factors in column purification process (Weber and Smith, 1987). Silica has a rigid structure therefore, unlike other absorbents do not alter the functionality of the conjugating agent that is the CD. Also, silica is resistive to chemical and thermal degradation hence, it can be used in a varied range of pH without major alternation in the properties. These advantages favoured its conjugation to CD and in subsequent adsorption of metal ions. To assess the feasibility of generated silica-CD nanoconjugates in the removal of heavy metal ions in real industrial effluent samples, column purification strategy was employed. The initial pH of the industrial effluent was 3.83.

After completion of column flow treatment with respective metal stock solutions, the different fractions were collected and subjected to AAS analysis. The adsorption data for the respective metal ion and industrial effluent are summarized in Fig. 2 . Higher efficiency of removal in silica-CD adsorbent was observed compared to the control set which had silica only, suggesting the adsorption of heavy metals (Ni and Fe) to CD surface. Further, the results signify higher removal for Fe compared to Ni, which is probably due to the high average valence electron energy of Fe compared to Ni (Hadi et al., 2013), i.e. one Fe3+ molecule can bind with 3 molecules of CD while a Ni2+ ion conjugates with two CD. Thus, the adsorption ratio will be higher for Fe compared to Ni. After treatment, the average concentration of Fe and Ni is observed to be 0.21 μmol/mL and 0.13 μmol/mL respectively. On calculating the average removal efficiency, 77% and 74% removal was observed for Fe and Ni respectively when eluted individually. However, when analysed the Ni and Fe heavy metal mixture, approx. 87% and 75% removal or Fe and Ni was found respectively. While the control set shows very less removal efficacy.

Meanwhile, the removal efficiency for industrial effluent was also determined. The removal efficiency was determined to be 74.80% and 79.47% for Fe and Ni respectively (Fig. 2). 357 mg g−1 Ni (II) adsorption capacity has been reported on magnetic sol–gel silica-based hybrid adsorbent (Ahmad et al., 2019). However, there are no studies on the use of silica nanocomposite’s as adsorbers for Fe as of yet. There was minimal interface by other metal ions present in the solution, signifying that the synthesized CD has a strong binding affinity to Ni and Fe. Zeta potential analysis had suggested that the CD (pH 6.97) has an average surface charge of −25 mV and hence it has a higher efficacy of binding to positively charged heavy metal ions. Thus, it signifies that the electrostatic interaction is responsible for the adsorption of Ni and Fe with the Silica-CD nanoconjugates.

Highly stable and photoluminescent CD were synthesized from leaf extract of Ficus benghalensis. CD showed superior selectivity towards Ni and Fe in a dose-dependent manner. Si-CD conjugates were generated to establish a column purification method that showed high efficacy in the removal of Fe and Ni from industrial effluents. The conjugates showed three and a twofold increase in Fe and Ni adsorption respectively. Electrostatic interaction of Fe and Ni with CD results in their immobilization on column bed of Si-CD conjugates. The substrate has a promising application in the establishment of continuous bed system for large scale water purification.

M.P. Ajith: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing, Visualization. Eepsita Priyadarshini: Formal analysis, Conceptualization, Validation, Resources, Writing - review & editing, Visualization. Paulraj Rajamani: Supervision, Conceptualization, Validation, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support from Department of Science and Technology (DST), New Delhi, India through INSPIRE fellowship is thankfully acknowledged. Authors are thankful to Advanced Instrumentation Research Facility (AIRF) of Jawaharlal Nehru University, New Delhi for allowing us to use TEM facility for characterization.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biortech.2020.123786.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
